clinicalStem: A 12-month-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic for her routine well-child visit. She was born at term, has received all prior vaccinations on schedule, and her mother has no concerns today. The pediatrician is reviewing which vaccines are due. The child has no known allergies and is not currently unwell. Which of the following is the most appropriate vaccination to administer today in order to prevent measles, mumps, and rubella?
options: DTaP (diphtheria, tetanus, acellular pertussis)
MMR (measles, mumps, rubella)
Varicella (VZV) vaccine alone
Hepatitis A vaccine
Inactivated polio vaccine (IPV)
explanation: The MMR vaccine is routinely administered at 12–15 months as the first dose, with a second dose at 4–6 years. It confers protection against measles, mumps, and rubella through live-attenuated virus. DTaP and IPV are given in earlier infancy. Hepatitis A is also started around 12 months but does not protect against measles.
wrongAnswerExplanations: DTaP protects against diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis; it does not prevent measles and is primarily given in the first 6 months of life.
Correct — MMR is the recommended vaccine for measles, mumps, and rubella prevention, first given at 12–15 months.
Varicella vaccine alone does not protect against measles, mumps, or rubella; MMRV combines both but administering varicella alone is not the answer here.
Hepatitis A vaccine starts at 12 months but has no effect on measles prevention.
IPV is given in infancy (2, 4, 6–18 months) and protects against poliovirus, not measles.
clinicalPearl: MMR is a live-attenuated vaccine given at 12–15 months (first dose) and 4–6 years (second dose); it must not be given to immunocompromised children.
keyClue: 12-month-old girl at routine well-child visit needing measles, mumps, rubella protection
commonExamTrap: Confusing DTaP (due at same age but for different pathogens) with MMR
missedConcept: MMR vaccination timing and its specific disease targets

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of low-grade fever and a generalized pruritic rash. On examination, the rash consists of crops of vesicles on an erythematous base in varying stages of development, involving the face, trunk, and extremities. He has no past medical history. His mother asks whether any medication is needed. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of his varicella infection?
options: Oral acyclovir 20 mg/kg/dose four times daily for 5 days
Intravenous acyclovir 10 mg/kg every 8 hours
Oral valacyclovir for 7 days
Supportive care only (antipyretics, calamine lotion, antihistamines)
Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for 5 days
explanation: Varicella in otherwise healthy children is a self-limiting disease and routine antiviral therapy is not recommended. Supportive care with antipyretics (avoiding aspirin to prevent Reye syndrome), topical calamine, and antihistamines for itch is standard. Oral acyclovir is reserved for high-risk patients (immunocompromised, neonates, adolescents >12 years, those on steroids, or secondary household cases). IV acyclovir is for severe/complicated varicella.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral acyclovir is not routinely recommended for healthy children with uncomplicated chickenpox; it is reserved for high-risk groups.
IV acyclovir is indicated only for severe or complicated varicella (encephalitis, pneumonia) or immunocompromised patients — not a routine healthy child.
Valacyclovir has no pediatric license for chickenpox in young children and is not first-line in this scenario.
Correct — supportive care is the standard management for uncomplicated varicella in an immunocompetent child.
Steroids are contraindicated in varicella as they can worsen the infection and increase risk of complications.
clinicalPearl: In healthy children under 12 with uncomplicated chickenpox, supportive care is sufficient; avoid aspirin (Reye syndrome risk) and steroids.
keyClue: Immunocompetent 9-year-old with classic vesicular rash in crops — no high-risk features
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oral acyclovir to every child with chickenpox — it is only indicated in high-risk patients
missedConcept: Antiviral indication criteria in varicella are risk-stratified, not routine

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks gestation attends her antenatal clinic. She reports a 2-day history of painful genital vesicles. This is identified as her first clinical episode of genital herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection. She is otherwise well. Fetal monitoring is reassuring. Which of the following is the most appropriate management to reduce the risk of neonatal herpes?
options: Allow vaginal delivery and start neonatal IV acyclovir prophylactically after birth
Administer maternal oral acyclovir 400 mg three times daily from now until delivery and plan caesarean section
Administer maternal oral acyclovir from 36 weeks only and plan vaginal delivery
Reassure the mother — neonatal herpes transmission only occurs with recurrent lesions
Administer maternal IV foscarnet and plan caesarean section
explanation: Primary genital HSV in the third trimester carries the highest risk of neonatal herpes (up to 50%) due to high viral shedding and absence of maternal antibodies. Guidelines recommend both antiviral suppression with acyclovir (400 mg TDS from diagnosis to delivery) AND caesarean section to minimise neonatal exposure. Oral acyclovir from 36 weeks alone is the strategy for recurrent HSV, not primary infection at term.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vaginal delivery with active primary HSV lesions carries unacceptably high transmission risk; caesarean section is indicated.
Correct — maternal oral acyclovir started immediately plus planned caesarean section is the recommended approach for primary HSV at term.
Acyclovir from 36 weeks is recommended for recurrent HSV suppression; primary HSV at 38 weeks requires immediate antiviral therapy AND caesarean section.
This is incorrect — primary HSV near term has higher transmission risk than recurrent episodes because maternal antibodies are absent.
Foscarnet is a second-line agent for acyclovir-resistant HSV; it is not indicated here and has significant toxicity.
clinicalPearl: Primary genital HSV in the third trimester requires immediate acyclovir plus caesarean section — unlike recurrent HSV where only suppression from 36 weeks is needed.
keyClue: First clinical episode of genital HSV at 38 weeks — primary infection near term with highest neonatal transmission risk
commonExamTrap: Applying the 'recurrent HSV' acyclovir-from-36-weeks rule to a primary infection
missedConcept: Primary vs recurrent HSV near term have different management algorithms
redFlagReason: Primary genital HSV at term risks neonatal HSV encephalitis and disseminated disease — a potentially fatal neonatal emergency requiring immediate preventive action

clinicalStem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a 4-day history of fever (38.8°C), productive cough, and right-sided chest pain. Chest X-ray shows right lower lobe consolidation. He is alert, maintaining oxygen saturation at 97% on room air, and tolerating oral fluids. He has no known drug allergies. Which antibiotic is the most appropriate first-line empirical treatment for community-acquired pneumonia in this child?
options: Co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) orally
Oral amoxicillin
Intravenous ceftriaxone
Oral azithromycin
Oral co-trimoxazole
explanation: In school-age children with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the most common bacterial cause. High-dose oral amoxicillin is the recommended first-line empirical antibiotic for non-severe CAP in immunocompetent children, as it targets pneumococcus effectively and has a favourable safety profile. The child is clinically stable with good oxygenation, so IV therapy is not yet warranted.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Co-amoxiclav is broader-spectrum than needed and is not recommended as first-line for typical CAP; it is used when aspiration or a resistant organism is suspected.
Correct — oral amoxicillin (high-dose) is the first-line empirical antibiotic for non-severe CAP in school-age children targeting Streptococcus pneumoniae.
IV ceftriaxone is reserved for severe CAP, children who cannot tolerate oral medication, or those who fail oral therapy.
Azithromycin targets atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila) and is used when an atypical cause is suspected or as second-line; it is not first-line for typical pneumococcal CAP.
Co-trimoxazole is not a standard antibiotic for CAP in children in most guidelines.
clinicalPearl: High-dose oral amoxicillin is the first-line antibiotic for non-severe community-acquired pneumonia in children; add a macrolide only if atypical infection is suspected.
keyClue: School-age child with lobar consolidation, stable oxygenation, tolerating orally — non-severe CAP
commonExamTrap: Choosing azithromycin as first-line, which only covers atypical organisms, not Streptococcus pneumoniae
missedConcept: Amoxicillin — not azithromycin — is first-line for typical bacterial CAP in children

clinicalStem: A 5-year-old girl is referred by her school nurse after it was discovered that her father was recently diagnosed with active pulmonary tuberculosis. The child is asymptomatic and BCG-vaccinated at birth. A Mantoux tuberculin skin test (TST) is performed; at 72 hours the induration measures 8 mm. Chest X-ray is normal and she has no respiratory symptoms. How should this TST result be interpreted?
options: Negative — the BCG vaccine always causes induration over 10 mm and this is below the threshold
Positive — a result ≥5 mm in a close contact of active TB is significant regardless of BCG status
Indeterminate — repeat testing in 8 weeks is required before any action
Negative — only a result ≥15 mm is positive in BCG-vaccinated children
Positive only if ≥10 mm, so this result is negative and no further action is needed
explanation: In children who are close contacts of active tuberculosis, the TST threshold for a positive result is ≥5 mm of induration, regardless of BCG vaccination status. An 8 mm induration in a child with a household contact of active TB is therefore a positive TST and warrants further investigation for latent TB infection (LTBI) and consideration of preventive therapy. BCG vaccination does not alter the ≥5 mm threshold in high-risk contacts.
wrongAnswerExplanations: BCG vaccination does not reliably produce >10 mm induration beyond a few years of vaccination and does not negate a positive result in a high-risk contact.
Correct — ≥5 mm induration is the positive threshold for close contacts of active TB, immunocompromised children, or those with radiographic evidence of TB.
There is no need to repeat the TST before acting in a symptomatic or high-risk contact; a positive result warrants prompt evaluation.
A 15 mm threshold applies to children with no known risk factors; for close contacts the threshold is ≥5 mm.
10 mm threshold applies to children from endemic countries or with high-risk exposures (not close household contact of active TB); the correct threshold here is 5 mm.
clinicalPearl: In close contacts of active TB, a TST induration ≥5 mm is positive regardless of BCG vaccination — the threshold is lower because pre-test probability is high.
keyClue: Household contact of active pulmonary TB — a high-risk exposure requiring a lower TST threshold of ≥5 mm
commonExamTrap: Applying the ≥10 mm or ≥15 mm threshold to a high-risk contact instead of the ≥5 mm contact threshold
missedConcept: TST positivity thresholds are risk-stratified: 5 mm for contacts/immunocompromised, 10 mm for high-prevalence groups, 15 mm for low-risk populations

clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He has a 6-hour history of high fever, irritability, and refusal to eat. On arrival he is drowsy (GCS 11/15), has a temperature of 39.6°C, heart rate 145 bpm, and a non-blanching petechial rash spreading over his trunk and limbs. Neck stiffness is noted. Capillary refill time is 3 seconds. What is the single most important immediate management step?
options: Obtain blood cultures and lumbar puncture before starting any treatment
Administer IV ceftriaxone immediately without waiting for lumbar puncture results
Perform CT head first, then lumbar puncture, then antibiotics
Start IV dexamethasone alone as a temporising measure
Administer IM adrenaline and oxygen only
explanation: This child has clinical features of bacterial meningitis with meningococcal septicaemia (non-blanching rash, meningism, haemodynamic compromise). The most critical immediate action is IV ceftriaxone — any delay in antibiotic administration worsens outcome. Blood cultures should ideally be taken immediately before antibiotics but must not delay treatment. LP is contraindicated until the child is stabilised and raised ICP has been excluded. Dexamethasone is an adjunct, not the primary treatment.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Blood cultures should be drawn rapidly but lumbar puncture must not precede antibiotics in a child with signs of raised ICP, sepsis, or coagulopathy — delay risks death.
Correct — immediate IV ceftriaxone is the highest priority; in meningococcal disease every minute of antibiotic delay increases mortality.
CT before LP before antibiotics is a dangerous sequence in a haemodynamically compromised child with meningism; antibiotics must not be delayed for imaging.
Dexamethasone reduces meningeal inflammation but has no antibacterial action; it is an adjunct given with or after the first antibiotic dose, not before.
IM adrenaline is indicated for anaphylaxis, not bacterial meningitis with septicaemia.
clinicalPearl: In suspected bacterial meningitis with non-blanching rash and haemodynamic compromise, administer IV ceftriaxone immediately — do not wait for LP or CT results.
keyClue: Non-blanching petechial rash + meningism + haemodynamic instability = meningococcal septicaemia — immediate antibiotics are life-saving
commonExamTrap: Delaying antibiotics to perform LP first — lumbar puncture is contraindicated in raised ICP and shock; antibiotics always come first
missedConcept: Antibiotic delay is the most common preventable cause of death in bacterial meningitis
redFlagReason: Meningococcal septicaemia with haemodynamic compromise and non-blanching rash — a paediatric emergency with high mortality if antibiotics are delayed

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old unvaccinated boy is brought to the emergency department by his father. Over the past 3 hours he has developed high fever (40.1°C), severe sore throat, drooling, and increasing respiratory distress. He is sitting forward in a 'tripod' position with his neck extended. His voice is muffled and he appears anxious and pale. Inspiratory stridor is present. What is the single most important next step in management?
options: Perform direct laryngoscopy immediately at the bedside to confirm the diagnosis
Order a lateral neck X-ray as the first investigation
Secure the airway urgently in a controlled setting (operating theatre) with senior anaesthetic and ENT support
Administer nebulised adrenaline and dexamethasone immediately
Obtain a throat swab for culture and start oral amoxicillin
explanation: This presentation is classic for acute epiglottitis — high fever, toxic appearance, drooling, tripod positioning, muffled voice, and inspiratory stridor in an unvaccinated child. The most critical step is securing the airway under controlled conditions in the operating theatre with anaesthetic and ENT support. Any procedure that may agitate the child (bedside laryngoscopy, throat swab, IV cannulation) can precipitate complete airway obstruction and cardiorespiratory arrest. Lateral neck X-ray ('thumbprint sign') may support the diagnosis but must not delay definitive airway management.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bedside laryngoscopy can precipitate complete airway obstruction by further agitating the child — it is absolutely contraindicated in suspected epiglottitis.
Lateral neck X-ray may show the thumbprint sign but agitating the child for investigations delays definitive airway management; imaging is secondary.
Correct — urgent controlled intubation in theatre with a senior anaesthetist and ENT surgeon is the priority; do not agitate the child before the airway is secured.
Nebulised adrenaline and dexamethasone are treatments for croup, not epiglottitis; they will not adequately treat the rapidly swelling epiglottis.
A throat swab risks gagging the child and precipitating airway obstruction; oral antibiotics are insufficient for acute epiglottitis requiring IV therapy.
clinicalPearl: Suspected epiglottitis is an airway emergency — keep the child calm, do not agitate, and secure the airway in a controlled theatre setting before any investigations.
keyClue: Tripod positioning, drooling, muffled voice, stridor in an unvaccinated child = epiglottitis until proven otherwise
commonExamTrap: Performing bedside laryngoscopy or throat examination in suspected epiglottitis — this can cause fatal airway obstruction
missedConcept: Epiglottitis is a 'do not touch' emergency; airway security precedes all investigations
redFlagReason: Acute epiglottitis with impending complete airway obstruction — any agitation or uncontrolled instrumentation can cause sudden cardiorespiratory arrest

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old girl is brought by her mother to the GP with a 2-day history of fever (38.5°C), right ear pain, and irritability. On otoscopy, the right tympanic membrane is erythematous, bulging, and has lost its normal light reflex. She has no known drug allergies, no prior ear infections, and is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic treatment?
options: Oral co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for 10 days
Oral amoxicillin for 5–7 days
Oral azithromycin for 3 days
Withhold antibiotics and review in 48–72 hours only
Topical ciprofloxacin ear drops
explanation: Acute otitis media (AOM) in children under 2 years with a bulging tympanic membrane and fever should be treated with antibiotics, as this age group has higher risk of complications. Oral amoxicillin is the first-line antibiotic (covers Streptococcus pneumoniae and non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae). Co-amoxiclav is reserved for failure of first-line treatment or known beta-lactamase producing organisms. Watchful waiting is acceptable in older children with mild AOM but not in those under 2 with bilateral disease or systemic symptoms.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Co-amoxiclav is second-line, used when amoxicillin fails or beta-lactamase-producing organisms are suspected; it is not the first-line choice.
Correct — oral amoxicillin for 5–7 days is the first-line antibiotic for AOM in children under 2 with a bulging tympanic membrane.
Azithromycin is a second-line option for penicillin-allergic patients; it is not the preferred first-line agent for AOM.
A 'watchful waiting' approach without antibiotics may be appropriate in children over 2 with mild unilateral AOM, but not in a child under 2 with fever and a bulging drum.
Topical ciprofloxacin ear drops are used for otitis externa or AOM with tympanostomy tubes — they do not treat middle ear infection through an intact tympanic membrane.
clinicalPearl: Amoxicillin is the first-line antibiotic for AOM in children under 2 years; watchful waiting is reserved for older children with mild, unilateral disease.
keyClue: Age under 2 with fever, bulging tympanic membrane — antibiotic treatment is indicated
commonExamTrap: Applying watchful waiting to all AOM cases — it is not appropriate for children under 2 years with bilateral AOM or systemic features
missedConcept: Age and severity stratify AOM management; under 2 years with a bulging drum warrants immediate antibiotics

subtopic: UTI in febrile infants — when to investigate
clinicalStem: A 7-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 2-day history of fever reaching 39.2°C and irritability. He has no cough, rash, or diarrhoea. On examination he appears unwell but is not septic. Physical examination reveals no obvious source of infection. Which of the following is the most important investigation to perform next?
options: Full blood count and CRP only
Chest X-ray
Urine microscopy and culture via clean-catch or catheter specimen
Blood culture and await results before further investigation
Throat swab for culture
explanation: Urinary tract infection (UTI) is the most common serious bacterial infection in febrile infants under 2 years with no identifiable source. Boys are more commonly affected in the first 6 months of life due to anatomical factors. A urine specimen for microscopy and culture (clean-catch or catheter specimen — not bag urine, which has high contamination rates) is the most important investigation to exclude UTI. Missing a UTI in an infant risks renal scarring and pyelonephritis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: FBC and CRP are useful inflammatory markers but do not diagnose UTI; urine culture is required to identify the causative organism.
Chest X-ray is not the priority in a febrile infant without respiratory symptoms; a urine source is more likely.
Correct — urine microscopy and culture is the most critical investigation to exclude UTI, the commonest serious bacterial infection in febrile infants with no obvious source.
Blood culture is important if bacteraemia is suspected but does not replace urine culture; urine testing is the more targeted investigation here.
Throat swab is not indicated in the absence of upper respiratory symptoms and would not account for the fever with no obvious source.
clinicalPearl: Any febrile infant under 2 years with no obvious source must have urine microscopy and culture performed — UTI is the most common serious bacterial infection in this age group.
keyClue: 7-month-old boy with fever and no identifiable source — UTI is the most likely serious bacterial infection
commonExamTrap: Only obtaining a bag urine sample, which has unacceptably high contamination rates — always use clean-catch or catheter specimen
missedConcept: UTI is the leading cause of serious bacterial infection in febrile infants and must be actively screened for with proper specimen collection

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman attends her antenatal clinic at 28 weeks of gestation. She has a healthy pregnancy and all antenatal blood work is normal. Her GP asks about pertussis prevention for the newborn. She mentions that her mother and partner have not been recently vaccinated. Which of the following is the single most effective strategy to protect the newborn from pertussis in the first months of life?
options: Vaccinate the newborn with DTaP at 2 weeks of birth
Administer Tdap to the mother during pregnancy (between 16 and 32 weeks)
Vaccinate only the father and grandmother immediately ('cocooning' strategy only)
Administer azithromycin prophylaxis to all household contacts after the baby is born
Advise strict hand hygiene alone as sufficient prevention
explanation: Maternal Tdap vaccination during pregnancy (optimally 16–32 weeks gestation) is the most effective strategy for protecting the newborn against pertussis. Maternal antibodies cross the placenta and provide passive immunity to the infant before primary vaccination begins. The DTaP primary series starts at 6–8 weeks; neonates are most vulnerable before this. Cocooning (vaccinating household contacts) is a complementary strategy but is less effective than maternal immunisation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: DTaP is not given at 2 weeks of age; the earliest dose is at 6–8 weeks, which leaves a window of vulnerability in the neonatal period.
Correct — maternal Tdap in pregnancy (16–32 weeks) allows transplacental antibody transfer, providing passive neonatal immunity before active vaccination begins.
Vaccinating household contacts (cocooning) is useful but cannot match the direct neonatal protection achieved by transplacental antibody transfer from maternal vaccination.
Azithromycin prophylaxis is used for post-exposure chemoprophylaxis in contacts, not as primary prevention for a whole household.
Hand hygiene alone is insufficient to prevent pertussis transmission, which is spread by respiratory droplets.
clinicalPearl: Maternal Tdap vaccination at 16–32 weeks of pregnancy provides transplacental antibody transfer and is the most effective way to protect neonates from pertussis before their own vaccination series begins.
keyClue: Antenatal visit at 28 weeks, need to protect newborn against pertussis before vaccination can start
commonExamTrap: Choosing cocooning alone over maternal vaccination — maternal immunisation is superior because it gives the newborn direct passive immunity
missedConcept: The primary vaccination series starts too late to protect neonates; transplacental antibodies from maternal Tdap fill this window

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old boy with known moderate asthma presents in January with a 36-hour history of abrupt-onset fever (39.4°C), myalgia, headache, and dry cough. His mother reports he was not vaccinated against influenza this season. He is alert, SpO2 97% on air, mildly wheezed, and appears ill. A rapid influenza antigen test is positive for influenza A. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Supportive care only — antivirals are not indicated in children over 5 years
Prescribe oral oseltamivir and continue usual asthma treatment
Administer IV zanamivir as oral therapy is ineffective in children
Start empirical oral amoxicillin for secondary bacterial pneumonia
Defer treatment and retest in 48 hours before prescribing antivirals
explanation: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) is recommended for children with influenza who have risk factors for severe disease, including asthma. It is most effective when started within 48 hours of symptom onset. This child has asthma, a confirmed influenza A infection, and symptoms for only 36 hours — all criteria for antiviral therapy. Continuing asthma management is also essential as influenza is a major asthma exacerbation trigger. Amoxicillin is not indicated without evidence of secondary bacterial infection.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Antivirals are recommended for influenza in high-risk children of any age, including those with asthma, chronic lung disease, immunosuppression, or young age.
Correct — oral oseltamivir within 48 hours of symptom onset plus continuation of asthma therapy is the appropriate management for this high-risk child.
IV zanamivir is reserved for severe/hospitalised cases or when oral oseltamivir cannot be used; oral oseltamivir is appropriate here.
There is no evidence of bacterial co-infection; empirical amoxicillin is not indicated at this stage.
Delaying antiviral treatment beyond 48 hours reduces its efficacy; treatment should begin promptly in a high-risk child with confirmed influenza.
clinicalPearl: Oseltamivir is indicated within 48 hours of symptom onset in children with influenza and high-risk conditions such as asthma, cardiac disease, or immunosuppression.
keyClue: Confirmed influenza A, asthma, symptom onset 36 hours ago — within the 48-hour antiviral window in a high-risk patient
commonExamTrap: Withholding oseltamivir from a child with asthma — high-risk conditions are the key indication for antiviral treatment in influenza
missedConcept: Antivirals are not for all children with influenza but ARE indicated for those with risk factors; asthma is a clear high-risk condition

topic: Neonatal group B streptococcal infection prevention
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman is in active labour at 39 weeks gestation. Antenatal Group B Streptococcus (GBS) vaginal/rectal swab at 35–37 weeks was positive. She has no documented penicillin allergy. She is 6 cm dilated. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate intervention to reduce the risk of early-onset neonatal GBS disease?
options: Administer intrapartum oral amoxicillin 500 mg 6-hourly until delivery
Administer intrapartum intravenous penicillin G and continue every 4 hours until delivery
Withhold antibiotics and observe the neonate closely after birth for 48 hours
Administer a single dose of IV ceftriaxone at the time of membrane rupture only
Proceed to emergency caesarean section to prevent vertical transmission
explanation: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) with IV penicillin G is the standard of care for GBS-colonised women to prevent early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis. IV penicillin G is preferred over ampicillin for GBS IAP due to its narrower spectrum and lower risk of promoting resistant organisms. It is given every 4 hours until delivery. Oral antibiotics are ineffective for IAP as adequate maternal serum and transplacental levels cannot be achieved. Caesarean section does not prevent GBS transmission and is not indicated solely for GBS carriage.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics do not achieve adequate serum levels for intrapartum prophylaxis; IV administration is mandatory for effective GBS prevention.
Correct — IV penicillin G every 4 hours during labour is the first-line intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for GBS-positive mothers, protecting the neonate from vertical transmission.
Observation alone after birth is insufficient if no intrapartum prophylaxis is given; untreated GBS colonisation carries a significant risk of neonatal sepsis, meningitis, and death.
A single dose is inadequate; penicillin G must be given continuously every 4 hours to maintain bactericidal levels throughout labour.
Caesarean section does not eliminate GBS transmission risk, as it can occur during rupture of membranes or via ascending infection; surgery is not indicated purely for GBS carriage.
clinicalPearl: IV penicillin G given every 4 hours during labour is the gold standard intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis for GBS-colonised mothers; it must be IV, not oral, to achieve adequate levels.
keyClue: GBS-positive swab at 35–37 weeks, now in active labour at 39 weeks — intrapartum prophylaxis indication is met
commonExamTrap: Choosing oral amoxicillin — oral antibiotics are ineffective for intrapartum GBS prophylaxis; IV penicillin G is required
missedConcept: IAP for GBS must be IV and repeated every 4 hours throughout labour — a single dose or oral route is not protective
redFlagReason: Early-onset GBS neonatal sepsis and meningitis are life-threatening; failure to administer correct intrapartum prophylaxis directly risks the neonate's life
